title: Ouden OS Plan
shortDescription: Windows optimization plan for Ouden OS
description: A review-first Windows optimization playbook that stages cleanup, privacy hardening, and performance tuning through a guided wizard.
details: Ouden OS scans the current install, resolves a profile-aware plan, and packages the same decisions for wizard and ISO flows.

bulletPoints.title: Lower Latency
bulletPoints.description: Stages the same timer, power, and scheduler optimizations exposed by the desktop wizard.

bulletPoints.title: Telemetry Control
bulletPoints.description: Removes the worst consumer tracking defaults without pretending every enterprise feature can disappear.

bulletPoints.title: Rollback Ready
bulletPoints.description: Keeps rollback metadata so the plan can be audited and reversed instead of acting like a blind tweak pack.

desktopQuestions.label: Optimization Level
desktopQuestions.title: How aggressively should we optimize your system?
desktopQuestions.desc: This preset controls which advanced questions appear later. Conservative is safe for anyone. Expert unlocks every tweak — including ones with real trade-offs.
desktopQuestions.note: You can always return and change this. Questions hidden at lower presets are just deferred, not locked.

options.title: Conservative — safe cleanup only
options.desc: Light cleanup and privacy fixes. Nothing that changes system behavior or touches security settings. Best for shared or work PCs.

options.title: Balanced — good for most systems
options.desc: A solid mix of performance, privacy, and stability improvements. Avoids anything that needs manual reversal.

options.title: Aggressive — gaming / enthusiast
options.desc: Unlocks deeper tweaks: memory compression, core parking, GPU P-state locks. Aimed at dedicated gaming rigs.

options.title: Expert — full control
options.desc: Every question is shown. Includes Spectre/Meltdown mitigation toggles, full Windows Update control, and Defender disablement. You accept the risks.

warnings: Expert preset enables questions that can significantly reduce system security. Only proceed if you understand the implications.

desktopQuestions.label: Power Plan
desktopQuestions.title: Switch to the High Performance power plan?
desktopQuestions.desc: The default Balanced plan throttles CPU speed to save power. High Performance keeps the CPU at full clock, eliminating latency spikes. performance research calls this the single most impactful change for gaming responsiveness.
desktopQuestions.note: HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Power — sets the active plan GUID to 8c5e7fda-e8bf-4a96-9a85-a6e23a8c635c.

options.title: Yes — switch to High Performance
options.desc: Full CPU clock at all times. Eliminates frequency ramp-up latency that causes frame-time spikes in games.

options.title: No — keep Balanced
options.desc: Windows default. Good for laptops on battery. CPU may throttle during bursty workloads.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep the Balanced power plan.

desktopQuestions.label: Fast Startup
desktopQuestions.title: Disable Fast Startup (Hybrid Boot)?
desktopQuestions.desc: Fast Startup saves a hibernation snapshot of the kernel on shutdown. This can cause stale drivers, hidden power states, and misleading 'uptime' readings. Both performance research and community benchmarks recommend disabling it for clean boots.
desktopQuestions.note: HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Session Manager\Power — HiberbootEnabled = 0.

options.title: Yes — disable Fast Startup
options.desc: Full cold boot on every shutdown. Slightly longer POST, but drivers always load fresh and hibernation snapshots can't interfere.

options.title: No — keep Fast Startup
options.desc: Faster Windows shutdown/resume cycle. May cause driver state issues after updates.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep Fast Startup enabled.

desktopQuestions.label: Hibernation
desktopQuestions.title: Disable hibernation and remove hiberfil.sys?
desktopQuestions.desc: Hibernation writes a full RAM snapshot to disk (hiberfil.sys). On a 16 GB+ system this file can be 8–16 GB. If you never hibernate, this is wasted space and pointless I/O on shutdown. performance research runs `powercfg /h off` as a standard step.
desktopQuestions.note: Runs: powercfg /hibernate off — removes C:\hiberfil.sys and disables the hibernate power state.

options.title: Yes — disable hibernation
options.desc: Frees hiberfil.sys disk space and removes the hibernate power option. Fast Startup also stops working (which is already disabled above).

options.title: No — keep hibernation
options.desc: Useful on laptops where you close the lid for long periods. Preserves hiberfil.sys.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep hibernation enabled.

desktopQuestions.label: Search Indexing
desktopQuestions.title: Disable Windows Search indexing (WSearch service)?
desktopQuestions.desc: The Windows Search service continuously indexes files in the background, consuming CPU and disk I/O. On an SSD where file access is already fast, the index provides minimal benefit while generating steady background load. Both performance research and community benchmarks recommend disabling it.
desktopQuestions.note: Sets WSearch service to disabled and stops it. HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\WSearch — Start = 4.

options.title: Yes — disable indexing
options.desc: Stops background I/O from the indexer. Search still works, just without pre-built index (slightly slower first results in Explorer).

options.title: No — keep indexing
options.desc: Instant results in Windows Search. Useful on HDDs or if you search files heavily.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep Windows Search indexing active.

desktopQuestions.label: Background App Access
desktopQuestions.title: Block apps from running in the background?
desktopQuestions.desc: Windows lets Store apps receive data, send notifications, and stay active when not focused. Disabling this via AppPrivacy policy reduces unnecessary CPU wake-ups and network calls. Note: on Windows 11 24H2, this setting moved into Explorer and may require a reboot to take effect.
desktopQuestions.note: HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\AppPrivacy — LetAppsRunInBackground = 2.

options.title: Yes — block background app activity
options.desc: Apps only run when focused. Reduces CPU wake-ups and network calls from idle apps. Effective after reboot on 24H2+.

warnings: On Windows 11 24H2+, this policy moved to Explorer. A reboot is required for the change to take full effect.

options.title: No — allow background app activity
options.desc: Apps can receive updates and notifications in the background. Default Windows behaviour.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to allow apps to run in the background.

desktopQuestions.label: SysMain / Superfetch
desktopQuestions.title: Disable SysMain (Superfetch) service?
desktopQuestions.desc: SysMain pre-loads frequently used apps into RAM to speed up launches. On an SSD, load times are already near-instant, so the service just burns RAM and generates background disk I/O with no real benefit. performance research marks this as a standard SSD optimisation.
desktopQuestions.note: HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\SysMain — Start = 4. The service is also stopped immediately.

options.title: Yes — disable SysMain
options.desc: Frees the RAM SysMain reserves for its cache. No practical downside on SSDs.

options.title: No — keep SysMain
options.desc: Useful on HDDs where pre-loading apps reduces perceptible launch lag.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep SysMain enabled.

desktopQuestions.label: Automatic Maintenance
desktopQuestions.title: Disable Windows Automatic Maintenance?
desktopQuestions.desc: Windows schedules maintenance tasks (disk defrag, Windows Defender scans, diagnostics) to run at 2 AM by default, but it can also trigger mid-session if the system was idle. This causes sudden CPU and disk spikes that hurt frame times. Both community benchmarks and performance research recommend disabling it.
desktopQuestions.note: HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Schedule\Maintenance — MaintenanceDisabled = 1.

options.title: Yes — disable automatic maintenance
options.desc: Prevents unexpected background scans and defrag runs. You can still trigger maintenance manually from Settings.

options.title: No — keep automatic maintenance
options.desc: Windows runs health tasks on a schedule. Can cause performance spikes if it triggers while you're gaming.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep automatic maintenance enabled.

desktopQuestions.label: Thread Scheduling
desktopQuestions.title: Optimize Win32PrioritySeparation for foreground responsiveness?
desktopQuestions.desc: Win32PrioritySeparation controls how much the scheduler boosts foreground thread priority over background threads. The default value (2) is balanced. performance research recommends 0x26 (38) which gives foreground threads a longer, higher-priority quantum — directly reducing input lag in games.
desktopQuestions.note: HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\PriorityControl — Win32PrioritySeparation = 38 (0x26). Also sets csrss.exe base priority to high.

options.title: Yes — boost foreground thread priority
options.desc: Foreground app (your game) gets longer CPU time slices and higher scheduler priority. Background tasks yield faster.

options.title: No — keep default scheduling
options.desc: Windows default balanced scheduler. Fine for multi-tasking workloads.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep the default Win32PrioritySeparation value.

desktopQuestions.label: Print Spooler
desktopQuestions.title: Do you use a printer on this PC?
desktopQuestions.desc: The Print Spooler service runs even when no printer is attached. If you don't print from this machine, disabling it removes an unnecessary service and closes several historical RPC attack surfaces.
desktopQuestions.note: HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\Spooler — Start = 4. This also disables the Fax service.

options.title: No printer — disable Print Spooler
options.desc: Stops the Spooler and Fax services. Printing will not work unless you re-enable the service.

options.title: Yes — keep Print Spooler
options.desc: Required for any local or network printing. Keep this if you use a printer.

behavior.blockReason: You indicated this PC uses a printer.

desktopQuestions.label: Remote Desktop / Phone Service
desktopQuestions.title: Do you use Remote Desktop or phone-linking on this PC?
desktopQuestions.desc: Remote Desktop and the Phone-Link (Phone Service) run background listeners. If you don't connect to this PC remotely or sync a phone, these services are pure overhead and attack surface.
desktopQuestions.note: Disables services.disable-remote-services and services.disable-phone-service.

options.title: No — disable remote services
options.desc: Stops Remote Desktop and Phone Service. You won't be able to RDP into this machine.

options.title: Yes — keep remote access
options.desc: Keeps Remote Desktop and Phone Link services running. Required for remote management or phone sync.

behavior.blockReason: You indicated this PC uses remote access or phone linking.

desktopQuestions.label: AutoPlay
desktopQuestions.title: Disable AutoPlay for removable drives?
desktopQuestions.desc: AutoPlay automatically runs content from USB drives and optical discs when inserted. This is a classic malware delivery vector and provides no real convenience benefit that File Explorer doesn't already cover. Both repos recommend disabling it.
desktopQuestions.note: HKCU\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Explorer\AutoplayHandlers — DisableAutoplay = 1.

options.title: Yes — disable AutoPlay
options.desc: Nothing happens automatically when a USB drive is plugged in. You open it manually via File Explorer.

options.title: No — keep AutoPlay
options.desc: Windows prompts you to open, play, or import content when a drive is plugged in.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep AutoPlay enabled.

desktopQuestions.label: NTFS Last Access Time
desktopQuestions.title: Disable NTFS last-access timestamp updates?
desktopQuestions.desc: By default, Windows writes a timestamp to every file it reads. On a busy system this creates a constant stream of small writes even during read-only operations. performance research recommends disabling this via `fsutil` for meaningful I/O reduction on mechanical drives and SSDs alike.
desktopQuestions.note: Runs: fsutil behavior set disablelastaccess 1 — also enables storage.ntfs-optimization for further NTFS tuning.

options.title: Yes — disable last-access writes
options.desc: Reading a file no longer triggers a metadata write. Reduces NTFS overhead, especially on HDDs.

options.title: No — keep last-access timestamps
options.desc: Every file read updates its last-access time. Useful for backup software that relies on access timestamps.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep NTFS last-access timestamps.

desktopQuestions.label: SVCHost Splitting
desktopQuestions.title: Set svchost process split threshold based on RAM?
desktopQuestions.desc: On systems with 4 GB+ RAM, Windows can run each Windows service in its own svchost.exe process. This isolates them for better stability and makes Task Manager readable. community benchmarks enables this by default on modern RAM amounts.
desktopQuestions.note: HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control — SvcHostSplitThresholdInKB = physical RAM in KB (e.g. 8388608 for 8 GB). Enables perf.svchost-split-threshold.

options.title: Yes — split services into individual processes
options.desc: Each service gets its own svchost.exe. Easier to diagnose CPU/memory hogs and slightly more crash-isolated.

options.title: No — keep grouped svchost
options.desc: Windows default: services share svchost processes. Slightly lower process count.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep the default svchost grouping.

desktopQuestions.label: Fault Tolerant Heap
desktopQuestions.title: Disable Fault Tolerant Heap (FTH)?
desktopQuestions.desc: FTH monitors apps that crash and silently applies compatibility shims to prevent them crashing again. The shims include artificial delays that cap performance — performance research has measured FTH secretly throttling game frame rates. If your apps are stable, FTH is pure overhead.
desktopQuestions.note: HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\FTH — Enabled = 0. This does NOT fix unstable apps; if an app needs FTH, disabling it will make the app crash more visibly.

options.title: Yes — disable FTH
options.desc: Removes the compatibility delay shims. Apps will crash normally instead of being silently throttled.

warnings: If a game or app relied on FTH shims, it may crash more often. Only disable if your system is stable.

options.title: No — keep FTH enabled
options.desc: Windows continues to protect unstable apps with compatibility shims. Safe default.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep Fault Tolerant Heap enabled.

desktopQuestions.label: Telemetry Level
desktopQuestions.title: How much Microsoft telemetry should be blocked?
desktopQuestions.desc: Windows sends diagnostic data to Microsoft continuously. The scope of what's acceptable differs by use case. Minimal blocks the DiagTrack service and the worst tracking. Standard adds advertising and activity feeds. Full Block adds every policy-controllable telemetry channel — including disabling DiagTrack, CEIP, TIPC, and telemetry scheduled tasks.
desktopQuestions.note: Minimal: services.disable-diagtrack. Standard adds privacy.disable-advertising-id, privacy.disable-activity-feed, privacy.disable-tailored-experiences. Full Block adds all remaining privacy.* and tasks.disable-telemetry-tasks actions.

options.title: Minimal — stop DiagTrack service only
options.desc: Disables the Connected User Experiences and Telemetry service (DiagTrack). Covers the highest-volume telemetry channel.

options.title: Standard — block advertising and activity tracking
options.desc: Adds advertising ID opt-out, activity feed, tailored experiences, and typing insights on top of the minimal block.

options.title: Full Block — disable every telemetry channel
options.desc: Blocks all of the above plus telemetry scheduled tasks, MSRT opt-out, online speech, device monitoring, and app launch tracking.

desktopQuestions.label: Activity Feed / Timeline
desktopQuestions.title: Disable Windows Activity Feed (Timeline)?
desktopQuestions.desc: The Activity Feed logs your app usage and document history to enable the Timeline feature. This data is also synced to Microsoft's servers if your account allows it. The feature was removed from the UI in Windows 11 but the logging layer still runs.
desktopQuestions.note: HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\System — EnableActivityFeed = 0, PublishUserActivities = 0.

options.title: Yes — disable Activity Feed
options.desc: Stops Windows from logging app and document history. Timeline (if present) will stop accumulating entries.

options.title: No — keep Activity Feed
options.desc: Windows logs your app activity. Required if you use Timeline or cross-device history features.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep the Activity Feed enabled.

desktopQuestions.label: Clipboard History
desktopQuestions.title: Disable Clipboard History and cloud sync?
desktopQuestions.desc: Clipboard History (Win+V) stores everything you copy. If cloud sync is enabled, this includes passwords and sensitive text sent to Microsoft's servers. community benchmarks and performance research both recommend disabling it unless you actively use the feature.
desktopQuestions.note: HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\System — AllowClipboardHistory = 0, AllowCrossDeviceClipboard = 0.

options.title: Yes — disable Clipboard History
options.desc: Clipboard works normally (copy/paste), but no history is stored locally or synced to the cloud.

options.title: No — keep Clipboard History
options.desc: Win+V lets you paste from previous clipboard entries. Useful for power users.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep Clipboard History enabled.

desktopQuestions.label: Location Services
desktopQuestions.title: Disable Location Services?
desktopQuestions.desc: Windows Location Services lets apps query your physical location. Unless you use maps or location-aware apps on this PC, this is unnecessary access to a privacy-sensitive sensor. community benchmarks marks this as an essential privacy tweak.
desktopQuestions.note: HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\LocationAndSensors — DisableLocation = 1.

options.title: Yes — disable Location Services
options.desc: No app (including Windows itself) can determine your physical location.

options.title: No — keep Location Services
options.desc: Apps can request your location. Required for maps, weather apps, and find-my-device.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep Location Services enabled.

desktopQuestions.label: Advertising ID
desktopQuestions.title: Opt out of the Windows Advertising ID?
desktopQuestions.desc: Windows assigns each user a unique advertising ID that apps use to track behaviour across sessions and deliver targeted ads. Opting out resets the ID and blocks new assignments. Both repos recommend this as a baseline privacy setting.
desktopQuestions.note: HKCU\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\AdvertisingInfo — Enabled = 0.

options.title: Yes — opt out of Advertising ID
options.desc: Apps can no longer read your advertising ID. Store apps and ad-funded software lose cross-app tracking capability.

options.title: No — keep Advertising ID
options.desc: Apps can use the ID to deliver targeted ads and track cross-app behaviour.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep the Advertising ID.

desktopQuestions.label: Tailored Experiences
desktopQuestions.title: Disable Tailored Experiences and diagnostic data usage?
desktopQuestions.desc: Tailored Experiences uses your diagnostic data to suggest Microsoft features, tips, and offers. It is separate from general telemetry — this specifically controls whether diagnostic data feeds into product recommendations sent back to your device.
desktopQuestions.note: HKCU\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Privacy — TailoredExperiencesWithDiagnosticDataEnabled = 0.

options.title: Yes — disable Tailored Experiences
options.desc: Microsoft stops using your diagnostic data to push product tips or feature suggestions.

options.title: No — allow tailored suggestions
options.desc: Microsoft may send personalised tips based on your diagnostic data.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to allow Tailored Experiences.

desktopQuestions.label: Online Speech Recognition
desktopQuestions.title: Disable Online Speech Recognition and personalisation?
desktopQuestions.desc: Online Speech Recognition sends your voice to Microsoft's cloud for recognition. Speech personalisation builds a model of your voice and vocabulary. Both are disabled by default in enterprise but left enabled for consumer accounts.
desktopQuestions.note: HKCU\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Speech_OneCore\Settings\OnlineSpeechPrivacy — HasAccepted = 0. Also sets privacy.disable-online-speech.

options.title: Yes — disable online speech features
options.desc: Voice commands still work locally, but nothing is sent to Microsoft's cloud for personalisation.

options.title: No — keep online speech features
options.desc: Microsoft builds a personalised speech model using your voice data.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep online speech personalisation.

desktopQuestions.label: Delivery Optimization
desktopQuestions.title: Disable Delivery Optimization (P2P update sharing)?
desktopQuestions.desc: Delivery Optimization can upload Windows Update data to other PCs on the internet, using your bandwidth. While it also helps you download updates faster from peers, on a metered or gaming connection the upload overhead is undesirable. Both repos recommend disabling it.
desktopQuestions.note: HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\DeliveryOptimization — DODownloadMode = 0.

options.title: Yes — disable Delivery Optimization
options.desc: Updates download from Microsoft servers only. No P2P upload of your cached update data.

options.title: No — keep P2P update sharing
options.desc: Windows may use your connection to seed updates to other PCs. Can speed up your own downloads on some networks.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep Delivery Optimization enabled.

desktopQuestions.label: Error Reporting
desktopQuestions.title: Disable Windows Error Reporting (WER)?
desktopQuestions.desc: When an app crashes, WER collects crash dumps and sends them to Microsoft. This is useful for developers but reveals crash information and app data to Microsoft on consumer machines. Disabling it also stops the WerSvc service from running.
desktopQuestions.note: HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\Windows Error Reporting — Disabled = 1. Also disables services.disable-wer-service.

options.title: Yes — disable error reporting
options.desc: Crash data stays local. No dumps are sent to Microsoft. Apps crash silently without triggering the WER dialog.

options.title: No — keep error reporting
options.desc: Crash dumps are sent to Microsoft. Useful if you're debugging software or want app vendors to get your crash reports.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep Windows Error Reporting enabled.

desktopQuestions.label: Consumer Cloud Content
desktopQuestions.title: Disable Consumer Features and cloud-pushed content?
desktopQuestions.desc: Windows Consumer Features installs suggested apps, adds promotional tiles to the Start menu, and enables cloud-delivered content like lock screen tips and spotlight ads. community benchmarks's DisableWindowsConsumerFeatures flag removes this layer entirely.
desktopQuestions.note: HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\CloudContent — DisableWindowsConsumerFeatures = 1. Also disables privacy.disable-cloud-content and tasks.disable-cloud-content-tasks.

options.title: Yes — disable cloud-pushed content
options.desc: No more suggested apps appearing in Start, no promotional tiles, no lock screen 'fun facts'.

options.title: No — allow cloud content
options.desc: Microsoft may push app suggestions and promotional content to Start and the lock screen.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to allow cloud-pushed consumer content.

desktopQuestions.label: Find My Device
desktopQuestions.title: Disable Find My Device?
desktopQuestions.desc: Find My Device requires location tracking to be active and transmits the PC's location to Microsoft. Unless you genuinely need to locate a lost laptop, this is unnecessary surveillance. community benchmarks includes this in its essential privacy block.
desktopQuestions.note: HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\FindMyDevice — AllowFindMyDevice = 0. Also applies privacy.disable-find-my-device.

options.title: Yes — disable Find My Device
options.desc: Location is not transmitted for device tracking. You won't be able to locate this PC via Microsoft account.

options.title: No — keep Find My Device
options.desc: Useful on laptops. Requires Location Services to be enabled.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep Find My Device enabled.

desktopQuestions.label: PowerShell Telemetry
desktopQuestions.title: Opt out of PowerShell telemetry?
desktopQuestions.desc: PowerShell 7+ sends command usage data to Microsoft by default unless the POWERSHELL_TELEMETRY_OPTOUT environment variable is set. community benchmarks includes this as an essential tweak.
desktopQuestions.note: Sets the POWERSHELL_TELEMETRY_OPTOUT=1 system environment variable. Applies to PS7+ only — Windows PowerShell 5.1 is unaffected.

options.title: Yes — opt out of PS telemetry
options.desc: PowerShell 7+ stops sending command analytics. The opt-out persists via a system environment variable.

options.title: No — keep PS telemetry
options.desc: PowerShell 7+ continues to report command usage to Microsoft.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep PowerShell telemetry.

desktopQuestions.label: Game Mode
desktopQuestions.title: Enable Windows Game Mode?
desktopQuestions.desc: Game Mode prioritises GPU and CPU resources for the foreground game, reduces Windows Update restarts during play, and (on supported hardware) enables variable refresh rate. Both repos recommend enabling it for gaming PCs.
desktopQuestions.note: HKCU\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\GameBar — AllowAutoGameMode = 1, AutoGameModeEnabled = 1.

options.title: Yes — enable Game Mode
options.desc: Windows prioritises the active game for CPU/GPU resources and defers background tasks during play.

options.title: No — keep default scheduling
options.desc: Games get no special scheduling priority over other apps.

behavior.blockReason: You chose not to enable Game Mode.

desktopQuestions.label: Game DVR / Background Recording
desktopQuestions.title: Disable Game DVR and background recording?
desktopQuestions.desc: Game DVR silently records your gameplay in the background at all times so you can save recent clips. The overhead is real — performance research and community benchmarks both flag GameDVR_Enabled as a must-disable for gaming. Disabling it also stops the Game Bar presence writer service.
desktopQuestions.note: HKCU\System\GameConfigStore — GameDVR_Enabled = 0. Also sets HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\GameDVR — AllowGameDVR = 0.

options.title: Yes — disable Game DVR
options.desc: No more background recording. Reduces CPU/GPU overhead during play and eliminates the background DVR thread.

options.title: No — keep Game DVR
options.desc: Xbox Game Bar can record recent gameplay with Win+Alt+G. Comes with background recording overhead.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep Game DVR and Game Bar.

desktopQuestions.label: Fullscreen Optimizations
desktopQuestions.title: Disable Fullscreen Optimizations (FSO)?
desktopQuestions.desc: Fullscreen Optimizations intercept exclusive fullscreen games and silently run them in a borderless window. This enables faster Alt+Tab and overlay injection but adds a compositor layer between the game and the display driver, increasing input latency. performance research recommends disabling FSO for exclusive fullscreen apps.
desktopQuestions.note: HKCU\System\GameConfigStore — GameDVR_FSEBehaviorMode = 2, GameDVR_DXGIHonorFSEWindowsCompatible = 1.

options.title: Yes — disable FSO
options.desc: Games run in true exclusive fullscreen mode. Lower input latency, but Alt+Tab is slower and some overlays may not work.

options.title: No — keep Fullscreen Optimizations
options.desc: Windows wraps exclusive fullscreen in a borderless window. Faster Alt+Tab, easier overlays.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep Fullscreen Optimizations enabled.

desktopQuestions.label: Legacy Flip / Presentation Mode
desktopQuestions.title: Enable legacy flip presentation mode for games?
desktopQuestions.desc: GameDVR_DXGIHonorFSEWindowsCompatible forces the D3D runtime to use the legacy flip presentation path when a game requests exclusive fullscreen. performance research uses this alongside disabling FSO to ensure the GPU driver handles presents directly without DWM involvement.
desktopQuestions.note: HKCU\System\GameConfigStore — GameDVR_DXGIHonorFSEWindowsCompatible = 1, GameDVR_EFSEFeatureFlags = 0. Companion to disableFullscreenOptimizations.

options.title: Yes — use legacy flip presentation
options.desc: D3D games that request exclusive fullscreen get the legacy flip path. Reduces present-to-display latency.

options.title: No — use default presentation path
options.desc: Windows chooses the presentation mode. May use DWM-based flip even in exclusive fullscreen.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to use the default presentation path.

desktopQuestions.label: Visual Transparency
desktopQuestions.title: Disable transparency effects in the UI?
desktopQuestions.desc: Transparency (acrylic, mica) requires the GPU to composite blurred samples of whatever is behind each window. It is a continuous GPU tax that exists purely for aesthetics. Both repos recommend disabling it to reclaim GPU headroom.
desktopQuestions.note: HKCU\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Themes\Personalize — EnableTransparency = 0.

options.title: Yes — disable transparency
options.desc: Taskbar, Start, and window chrome become opaque. Slightly lower GPU load in the desktop compositor.

options.title: No — keep transparency effects
options.desc: Acrylic and mica effects remain on taskbar and windows.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep transparency effects.

desktopQuestions.label: Mouse Acceleration
desktopQuestions.title: Disable mouse pointer acceleration?
desktopQuestions.desc: Pointer acceleration ("Enhance pointer precision") scales mouse movement speed non-linearly based on how fast you move the mouse. Modern games use raw input and bypass this entirely, but some older titles don't. Disabling it gives 1:1 physical-to-screen mapping at all speeds. performance research: 'most modern games use raw input anyway, but disabling ensures nothing interferes'.
desktopQuestions.note: HKCU\Control Panel\Mouse — MouseSpeed = 0, MouseThreshold1 = 0, MouseThreshold2 = 0.

options.title: Yes — disable pointer acceleration
options.desc: Raw 1:1 mouse tracking. Muscle memory is consistent at any speed. Required for competitive shooters.

options.title: No — keep pointer acceleration
options.desc: Windows default: pointer speed scales with physical movement speed. Feels natural for desktop use.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep pointer acceleration enabled.

desktopQuestions.label: Sticky Keys
desktopQuestions.title: Disable Sticky Keys and other accessibility interrupts?
desktopQuestions.desc: Sticky Keys, Toggle Keys, and Filter Keys are accessibility features that pop up dialogs when you press Shift 5 times or hold keys too long. In games this causes focus-stealing dialogs mid-play. Both repos include this as a standard gaming tweak.
desktopQuestions.note: HKCU\Control Panel\Accessibility\StickyKeys — Flags = 506. Also sets ToggleKeys and FilterKeys flags to suppress prompts.

options.title: Yes — disable Sticky/Toggle/Filter Keys prompts
options.desc: No more Sticky Keys dialogs during games. The keyboard shortcuts that trigger them are disabled.

options.title: No — keep accessibility key prompts
options.desc: Holding Shift 5 times still triggers the Sticky Keys dialog. Required if you use these accessibility features.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep Sticky Keys prompts enabled.

desktopQuestions.label: Memory Compression
desktopQuestions.title: Disable Windows Memory Compression?
desktopQuestions.desc: Memory Compression compresses in-RAM pages to fit more data in physical memory, using CPU cycles to compress and decompress. On systems with ample RAM (16 GB+), compression adds latency without saving anything useful. performance research recommends disabling it when you have sufficient RAM.
desktopQuestions.note: Runs: Disable-MMAgent -mc in PowerShell. Requires reboot. Only beneficial if RAM > 16 GB.

options.title: Yes — disable memory compression
options.desc: RAM pages are never compressed. Saves CPU cycles at the cost of slightly higher working set pressure. Best on 16 GB+ systems.

warnings: Only recommended on systems with 16 GB+ RAM. With less RAM, disabling compression may increase pagefile usage.

options.title: No — keep memory compression
options.desc: Windows compresses rarely-accessed pages to fit more in RAM. Good for 8 GB and under.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep memory compression enabled.

desktopQuestions.label: Core Parking
desktopQuestions.title: Disable CPU core parking?
desktopQuestions.desc: Core parking powers down CPU cores that aren't in heavy use to save energy. When a burst of work arrives, parked cores must wake up — adding latency spikes. performance research disables core parking via power plan configuration to keep all cores ready at all times.
desktopQuestions.note: Sets HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Power\PowerSettings — MinValueMax/MaxValueMax for core parking to 0 (0 parked cores).

options.title: Yes — disable core parking
options.desc: All CPU cores stay awake. Eliminates wake-up latency when workload bursts. Slightly higher idle power draw.

options.title: No — allow core parking
options.desc: Idle cores are powered down. Saves energy. May add latency on workload spikes.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to allow core parking.

desktopQuestions.label: CPU Boost Mode
desktopQuestions.title: Set CPU boost mode to Aggressive?
desktopQuestions.desc: CPU boost mode controls how eagerly the processor boosts clock speed above its base frequency. The default Efficient Aggressive setting can throttle boost on short bursts. performance research sets this to Aggressive (5) to ensure the CPU boosts immediately and stays there during gaming.
desktopQuestions.note: HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Power\PowerSettings\54533251-...\be337238-... — ValueMax = 5 (Aggressive).

options.title: Yes — aggressive boost mode
options.desc: CPU boosts immediately and stays at maximum boost frequency during load. Higher thermals and power draw.

warnings: Aggressive boost increases thermals. Ensure your CPU cooler can handle sustained boost frequencies.

options.title: No — keep default boost mode
options.desc: Windows manages boost aggressiveness automatically. Lower thermals.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep the default CPU boost mode.

desktopQuestions.label: MMCSS Gaming Profile
desktopQuestions.title: Apply MMCSS gaming profile and system responsiveness tuning?
desktopQuestions.desc: The Multimedia Class Scheduler Service (MMCSS) manages thread scheduling for multimedia and gaming workloads. performance research sets SystemResponsiveness to 0 (full CPU time to multimedia tasks), disables MMCSS throttling, and tunes the Games profile for minimum scheduling latency.
desktopQuestions.note: HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Multimedia\SystemProfile — SystemResponsiveness = 0, NetworkThrottlingIndex = 0xFFFFFFFF. Games sub-key: GPU Priority = 8, Priority = 6, Scheduling Category = High.

options.title: Yes — apply MMCSS gaming profile
options.desc: Scheduler gives games maximum multimedia priority. Reduces frame-time variance in CPU-bound scenarios.

options.title: No — keep default MMCSS settings
options.desc: Windows uses its default scheduling priorities for all workloads.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep the default MMCSS profile.

desktopQuestions.label: GPU Vendor Telemetry
desktopQuestions.title: Disable NVIDIA and AMD GPU telemetry services?
desktopQuestions.desc: NVIDIA Telemetry Container and AMD services like amdow collect hardware and usage data in the background. They run even when you're gaming and consume system resources. performance research documents both as unnecessary for gaming.
desktopQuestions.note: Disables gpu.disable-nvidia-telemetry, gpu.disable-nvidia-container, gpu.disable-amd-telemetry, gpu.disable-amd-services. Only the relevant vendor's services are touched based on installed GPU.

options.title: Yes — disable GPU vendor telemetry
options.desc: NVIDIA and AMD background reporting services are stopped and disabled. GPU drivers continue to function normally.

options.title: No — keep GPU vendor services
options.desc: NVIDIA and AMD telemetry services run in the background as installed.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep GPU vendor services running.

desktopQuestions.label: NVIDIA P-State Lock
desktopQuestions.title: Lock NVIDIA GPU to P-State 0 (maximum performance)?
desktopQuestions.desc: NVIDIA dynamic P-states ramp GPU clocks up and down based on load. During frame rendering the GPU can spend time in a lower P-state before detecting the load spike and boosting. Locking P-State 0 keeps the GPU at maximum clocks at all times, eliminating ramp-up latency. performance research documents this for desktop rigs.
desktopQuestions.note: Sets gpu.nvidia-disable-dynamic-pstate. Writes to NVIDIA driver registry keys under HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Class\{4d36e968-...}. Not recommended for laptops — causes thermal throttling on restricted cooling.

options.title: Yes — lock GPU to P0
options.desc: NVIDIA GPU stays at maximum clocks. No ramp-up latency. Higher idle power draw and temps.

warnings: Idle power consumption increases significantly. Not suitable for laptops or systems with weak cooling.

options.title: No — allow dynamic P-states
options.desc: GPU clocks scale with workload. Lower idle temps and power draw.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to allow dynamic GPU P-states.

desktopQuestions.label: Device Power Saving
desktopQuestions.title: Disable power saving on PCI/USB devices?
desktopQuestions.desc: Windows can put PCIe links and USB devices into low-power states when idle. The wake-up latency from these states adds jitter to input devices and network cards. performance research uses WMI to disable power management on all eligible devices.
desktopQuestions.note: Applies power.disable-device-power-saving, power.disable-usb-selective-suspend, power.disable-pcie-link-state-pm via WMI and power plan settings.

options.title: Yes — disable device power saving
options.desc: USB and PCIe devices stay fully powered. Eliminates device wake-up latency. Slightly higher system idle power.

options.title: No — allow device power management
options.desc: Windows puts idle devices to sleep to save power. Recommended for laptops.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep device power management enabled.

desktopQuestions.label: Search Web Noise
desktopQuestions.title: Remove Bing, search highlights, and suggestion history from Start?
desktopQuestions.desc: Windows Start search sends every keystroke to Bing by default and shows trending searches, news highlights, and history suggestions. This is telemetry, distracting, and slows search. Both repos include this as a baseline UX cleanup.
desktopQuestions.note: HKCU\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Search — BingSearchEnabled = 0, CortanaConsent = 0. Also applies shell.disable-search-highlights and shell.disable-search-history.

options.title: Yes — remove web search from Start
options.desc: Start search only searches local files and apps. No Bing, no trending searches, no history suggestions.

options.title: No — keep web search integration
options.desc: Start search includes Bing results, trending topics, and search history.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep Bing and web search in Start.

desktopQuestions.label: Microsoft Edge
desktopQuestions.title: What should we do about Microsoft Edge?
desktopQuestions.desc: Edge is the default browser and deeply integrated into Windows. It pre-loads itself at boot, shows ads in the new tab page, and cannot be uninstalled through normal means. You can suppress its aggressive behaviour, remove it (keeping WebView2 for app compatibility), or remove both.
desktopQuestions.note: Suppress: appx.disable-edge-updates, appx.disable-edge-preload, appx.disable-edge-default-nag. Remove: appx.remove-edge. Remove+WebView: also appx.remove-edge-webview.

options.title: Suppress — stop Edge pre-loading and ads
options.desc: Edge stays installed but stops pre-loading at startup and stops showing default-browser nags. Updates are paused.

options.title: Remove Edge — keep WebView2 for app compatibility
options.desc: Edge browser is removed. WebView2 runtime stays — required by many apps (Teams, Spotify, some games).

warnings: Some Windows features (PDF viewer, some Settings pages) use Edge. They may fall back to prompting you to install a browser.

options.title: Remove Edge + WebView2 — complete removal
options.desc: Both Edge and WebView2 are removed. Some apps that embed Edge for rendering will break.

warnings: Apps that use WebView2 for rendering (Spotify, Discord, some games) may fail to launch or crash after WebView2 removal.
Windows Update UI and some Settings pages may break. You will need an alternative browser already installed.

desktopQuestions.label: Copilot
desktopQuestions.title: Disable and remove Copilot?
desktopQuestions.desc: Copilot adds a persistent sidebar button to the taskbar and runs background services even when not in use. Both repos recommend removing it. This covers the Copilot taskbar button, the Copilot app package, and its autostart entries.
desktopQuestions.note: HKCU\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Explorer\Advanced — ShowCopilotButton = 0. Also removes appx.remove-copilot and appx.remove-copilot-app.

options.title: Yes — disable and remove Copilot
options.desc: Copilot button disappears from the taskbar and the app package is removed.

options.title: No — keep Copilot
options.desc: Copilot stays visible and accessible via the taskbar button.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep Copilot.

desktopQuestions.label: AI Features
desktopQuestions.title: Disable Recall, Click to Do, and AI app integrations?
desktopQuestions.desc: Windows 11 24H2+ ships Recall (continuous screenshot indexing), Click to Do (AI overlay on screen content), and AI-powered integrations in Notepad and Paint. These features continuously analyse your screen and files. performance research recommends disabling them entirely for privacy and performance.
desktopQuestions.note: Applies privacy.disable-recall, privacy.disable-click-to-do, privacy.disable-notepad-ai, privacy.disable-paint-ai, privacy.disable-edge-ai, privacy.disable-ai-svc-autostart, appx.remove-recall, privacy.block-app-reprovisioning.

options.title: Yes — disable AI screen features
options.desc: Recall, Click to Do, and in-app AI features are disabled and their autostart entries removed.

options.title: No — keep AI features
options.desc: Recall, Click to Do, and AI integrations remain active.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep AI features enabled.

desktopQuestions.label: Taskbar Cleanup
desktopQuestions.title: Clean up the taskbar — remove Widgets, Chat, Search box, and other clutter?
desktopQuestions.desc: The Windows 11 taskbar ships with Widgets, Chat (Teams), a large Search box, Task View button, and Meet Now — most of which are rarely used and occupy prime screen real estate. This removes the default clutter in one step.
desktopQuestions.note: Applies shell.hide-task-view, shell.hide-widgets-button, shell.reduce-search-box, shell.hide-chat-icon, shell.disable-meet-now, startup.disable-cloud-notifications.

options.title: Yes — remove taskbar clutter
options.desc: Widgets, Chat, Task View, Meet Now, and the Search box are hidden. Only pinned app icons remain.

options.title: No — keep default taskbar layout
options.desc: Widgets, Search, and Chat buttons remain as Microsoft shipped them.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep the default taskbar layout.

desktopQuestions.label: Context Menu Cleanup
desktopQuestions.title: Remove unused right-click context menu entries?
desktopQuestions.desc: The Windows 11 context menu ships with 'Cast to Device', 'Troubleshoot Compatibility', and 'Edit with Paint 3D' entries that appear on every file and folder. These are rarely used and slow down right-click rendering.
desktopQuestions.note: Removes shell handler registrations for Cast to Device, Troubleshoot Compatibility, and Paint 3D from the registry.

options.title: Yes — clean up context menu
options.desc: Cast to Device, Troubleshoot Compatibility, and Edit with Paint 3D are removed from right-click menus.

options.title: No — keep all context menu entries
options.desc: Right-click menus stay as Microsoft shipped them.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep all context menu entries.

desktopQuestions.label: Explorer Defaults
desktopQuestions.title: Improve File Explorer defaults — show extensions, open to This PC?
desktopQuestions.desc: Windows hides file extensions by default (a security risk — you can't see .exe disguised as .pdf), opens Explorer to Quick Access, and hides system files. These changes make Explorer more honest and useful.
desktopQuestions.note: HKCU\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Explorer\Advanced — HideFileExt = 0, Hidden = 1, LaunchTo = 1.

options.title: Yes — show extensions, open to This PC
options.desc: File extensions are always visible. Explorer opens to This PC. Hidden files are shown. Reduces confusion and phishing risk.

options.title: No — keep Windows defaults
options.desc: Extensions are hidden, Explorer opens to Quick Access.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep default Explorer settings.

desktopQuestions.label: Taskbar End Task
desktopQuestions.title: Enable End Task from the taskbar right-click menu?
desktopQuestions.desc: Windows 11 added an End Task option to the taskbar right-click menu (hidden behind a developer setting by default). community benchmarks enables it — it's a quality-of-life improvement that lets you kill a frozen window without opening Task Manager.
desktopQuestions.note: HKCU\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Explorer\Advanced\TaskbarDeveloperSettings — TaskbarEndTask = 1.

options.title: Yes — enable taskbar End Task
options.desc: Right-clicking a taskbar button now shows 'End Task' to immediately kill the process.

options.title: No — use Task Manager to end tasks
options.desc: No shortcut in the taskbar. Open Task Manager to terminate processes.

behavior.blockReason: You chose not to add End Task to the taskbar.

desktopQuestions.label: NetBIOS / LLMNR
desktopQuestions.title: Disable NetBIOS over TCP/IP and LLMNR?
desktopQuestions.desc: NetBIOS and LLMNR are legacy name-resolution protocols with well-documented attack vectors (LLMNR poisoning, NTLM relay). On a modern home or gaming network that uses DNS, these are pure attack surface. performance research recommends disabling both.
desktopQuestions.note: Sets NetBIOS option to disabled on all network adapters via WMI. Applies network.disable-llmnr via HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows NT\DNSClient — EnableMulticast = 0.

options.title: Yes — disable NetBIOS and LLMNR
options.desc: Legacy name resolution is off. Removes LLMNR poisoning attack surface. Name resolution falls back to DNS only.

options.title: No — keep legacy name resolution
options.desc: NetBIOS and LLMNR remain active. May be needed for very old network devices.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep NetBIOS and LLMNR.

desktopQuestions.label: IPv6
desktopQuestions.title: Disable IPv6 on all network adapters?
desktopQuestions.desc: IPv6 is not routed on most home ISPs and introduces a second network stack that adds overhead and edge-case bugs. community benchmarks includes this as an advanced tweak. Note: some VPNs and modern services rely on IPv6.
desktopQuestions.note: Sets DisabledComponents = 0xFF in HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\Tcpip6\Parameters. Requires reboot to fully take effect.

options.title: Yes — disable IPv6
options.desc: IPv6 is turned off. All traffic uses IPv4. Some VPNs and IPv6-only services will stop working.

warnings: Some VPN providers route traffic over IPv6. Disabling IPv6 may break these VPNs.
A reboot is required for the change to fully take effect.

options.title: No — keep IPv6 enabled
options.desc: Both IPv4 and IPv6 are active. Default Windows behaviour.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep IPv6 enabled.

desktopQuestions.label: Teredo Tunneling
desktopQuestions.title: Disable Teredo IPv6 tunneling?
desktopQuestions.desc: Teredo tunnels IPv6 traffic over IPv4 UDP. It is rarely needed on modern networks and keeps a persistent tunnel open to Microsoft's Teredo servers. community benchmarks marks it as an advanced cleanup item.
desktopQuestions.note: Runs: netsh interface teredo set state disabled. Also sets HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\Tcpip6\Parameters — DisabledComponents includes Teredo flag.

options.title: Yes — disable Teredo
options.desc: IPv6 tunneling is off. Closes the persistent tunnel to Microsoft's Teredo servers.

options.title: No — keep Teredo
options.desc: Teredo tunneling remains active. Required for some P2P and Xbox Live NAT traversal.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep Teredo tunneling.

desktopQuestions.label: Nagle Algorithm
desktopQuestions.title: Disable Nagle's algorithm for lower network latency?
desktopQuestions.desc: Nagle's algorithm buffers small TCP packets and combines them before sending. This reduces bandwidth overhead but adds latency — every small packet waits up to 200ms to be bundled. performance research disables it for gaming to ensure packets are sent immediately.
desktopQuestions.note: HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\Tcpip\Parameters\Interfaces\{adapter} — TcpAckFrequency = 1, TCPNoDelay = 1.

options.title: Yes — disable Nagle (lower latency)
options.desc: Small TCP packets are sent immediately. Reduces ping jitter in online games at the cost of slightly more packets.

options.title: No — keep Nagle enabled
options.desc: TCP coalesces small packets. Better for bulk transfers, adds small latency to interactive traffic.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep Nagle's algorithm enabled.

desktopQuestions.label: USB Selective Suspend
desktopQuestions.title: Disable USB Selective Suspend?
desktopQuestions.desc: USB Selective Suspend allows the USB hub to power down individual ports to save energy. When an input device (mouse, keyboard, controller) is on a suspended port, wake-up latency adds jitter to inputs. Disabling this keeps all USB ports fully powered.
desktopQuestions.note: Power plan setting: USB selective suspend setting = Disabled. Also applies power.disable-usb-selective-suspend.

options.title: Yes — disable USB Selective Suspend
options.desc: All USB ports stay fully powered. Input device wake latency is eliminated.

options.title: No — allow USB Selective Suspend
options.desc: Idle USB ports are powered down. Slightly better battery life on laptops.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep USB Selective Suspend.

desktopQuestions.label: PCIe Link State Power Management
desktopQuestions.title: Disable PCIe Link State Power Management?
desktopQuestions.desc: PCIe ASPM (Active State Power Management) allows the link between CPU and PCIe devices (GPU, NVMe, NICs) to drop to a low-power state when idle. Exiting ASPM adds latency. performance research disables it for desktop gaming rigs to keep all PCIe links at full speed.
desktopQuestions.note: Power plan setting: PCI Express > Link State Power Management = Off. Applies power.disable-pcie-link-state-pm.

options.title: Yes — disable PCIe ASPM
options.desc: PCIe links stay at full speed. Eliminates ASPM exit latency on GPU and NVMe requests.

options.title: No — allow PCIe power management
options.desc: PCIe links drop to low-power when idle. Recommended for laptops to improve battery life.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep PCIe Link State Power Management.

desktopQuestions.label: SmartScreen
desktopQuestions.title: Disable Microsoft SmartScreen?
desktopQuestions.desc: SmartScreen checks every downloaded file and URL against Microsoft's reputation database. This provides real malware protection but also sends file hashes and URLs to Microsoft. Disabling it removes the protection and the telemetry. Only do this if you rely on other security tools.
desktopQuestions.note: HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\System — EnableSmartScreen = 0. Also applies privacy.disable-smartscreen.

options.title: Yes — disable SmartScreen
options.desc: File reputation checks stop. No file hash or URL is sent to Microsoft for screening. You lose a real layer of malware protection.

warnings: SmartScreen blocks real malware. Disabling it removes a meaningful protection layer. Only do this if you have alternative endpoint protection.

options.title: No — keep SmartScreen
options.desc: Downloaded files and URLs are checked against Microsoft's cloud reputation database.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep SmartScreen enabled.

desktopQuestions.label: Windows Defender
desktopQuestions.title: What should happen with Windows Defender?
desktopQuestions.desc: Windows Defender provides real-time antivirus protection. It consumes CPU/RAM and can interfere with game file access. You can keep it, reduce its footprint by disabling cloud-based lookups, or disable it entirely — which performance research calls a significant security risk and marks as default FALSE.
desktopQuestions.note: Reduce: security.disable-defender-cloud (DisableBehaviorMonitoring, SpynetReporting=0). Full disable: security.full-defender-disable, security.disable-defender-realtime. Requires VBS/Tamper Protection to be off first.

options.title: Keep Defender — full protection
options.desc: Real-time protection, cloud lookups, and automatic sample submission all remain active.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep Defender fully active.

options.title: Reduce — disable cloud lookups and MAPS
options.desc: Local real-time scanning stays on. Cloud-based lookups and automatic sample submission are disabled — reducing telemetry without losing local scanning.

warnings: Cloud-based threat intelligence is disabled. Zero-day malware detected only by cloud lookups may not be caught.

options.title: Full Disable — turn off all real-time protection
options.desc: Defender is completely disabled. No real-time scanning of any kind.

warnings: You will have no antivirus protection. Malware, ransomware, and trojans will run undetected.
This requires Tamper Protection and VBS to already be disabled.
performance research labels this default: FALSE. Only do this with a third-party AV in place.

desktopQuestions.label: HVCI / Core Isolation
desktopQuestions.title: Disable HVCI (Hypervisor-Protected Code Integrity)?
desktopQuestions.desc: HVCI uses the hypervisor to protect the kernel from unsigned code injection. It is a strong security feature but adds overhead to system calls and is incompatible with some older game anti-cheats and kernel drivers. Both repos document this tradeoff.
desktopQuestions.note: HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\DeviceGuard\Scenarios\HypervisorEnforcedCodeIntegrity — Enabled = 0. Requires reboot.

options.title: Yes — disable HVCI
options.desc: Kernel code integrity is no longer hypervisor-protected. Enables older drivers and some anti-cheats. Reduces security significantly.

warnings: Disabling HVCI removes a key kernel integrity protection. Unsigned or malicious drivers can inject code into the kernel.

options.title: No — keep HVCI enabled
options.desc: Hypervisor protects the kernel from unsigned code. Slight system call overhead.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep HVCI enabled.

desktopQuestions.label: Virtualization Based Security
desktopQuestions.title: Disable Virtualization Based Security (VBS)?
desktopQuestions.desc: VBS uses the CPU hypervisor to create an isolated memory region for security features like Credential Guard and HVCI. It adds overhead to every VM-exit path and can reduce gaming frame rates by 5–15% on some CPUs. Both repos document this as an aggressive performance tradeoff.
desktopQuestions.note: HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\DeviceGuard — EnableVirtualizationBasedSecurity = 0. Requires reboot. HVCI must also be disabled first.

options.title: Yes — disable VBS
options.desc: Hypervisor-based isolation is off. Removes VM-exit overhead on hot paths. Disables Credential Guard and HVCI.

warnings: Disabling VBS removes Credential Guard and all hypervisor-based isolation features.
Pass-the-hash and credential theft attacks become easier without Credential Guard.
Requires HVCI to already be disabled.

options.title: No — keep VBS enabled
options.desc: Hypervisor isolation protects credentials and kernel integrity. Recommended for most users.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep VBS enabled.

desktopQuestions.label: SSBD Mitigation
desktopQuestions.title: Reduce Speculative Store Bypass (SSBD) mitigation?
desktopQuestions.desc: Speculative Store Bypass (CVE-2018-3639) mitigations add overhead to every system call. performance research documents reducing SSBD to the minimum required level for a measurable performance gain. This is an expert-only tweak — it meaningfully reduces the attack surface only in specific server or multi-tenant scenarios, not on gaming PCs.
desktopQuestions.note: Sets security.reduce-ssbd-mitigation — HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Session Manager\Memory Management — FeatureSettingsOverride/Mask for SSBD reduction.

options.title: Yes — reduce SSBD mitigation
options.desc: SSBD is set to the minimum required level. Slightly lower system call overhead. Only meaningful on multi-core workloads.

warnings: Reduces protection against Speculative Store Bypass in multi-tenant or sandboxed execution environments.
Not meaningful for a single-user gaming PC. Expert-only.

options.title: No — keep SSBD mitigation
options.desc: Full Speculative Store Bypass protection remains active.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep SSBD mitigation.

desktopQuestions.label: CPU Spectre/Meltdown Mitigations
desktopQuestions.title: Disable CPU Spectre and Meltdown mitigations?
desktopQuestions.desc: Spectre and Meltdown mitigations (Retpoline, KPTI, IBRS) add overhead to every system call and context switch. performance research documents this as a real performance gain on gaming PCs — but also as a genuine security risk. These mitigations block an entire class of side-channel CPU attacks.
desktopQuestions.note: Sets security.disable-cpu-mitigations — HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Session Manager\Memory Management — FeatureSettingsOverride = 3, FeatureSettingsOverrideMask = 3.

options.title: Yes — disable CPU mitigations
options.desc: Removes Spectre/Meltdown overhead from every syscall. Noticeable perf gain on older Intel CPUs. High security risk.

warnings: Disabling CPU mitigations exposes the system to Spectre v1/v2 and Meltdown side-channel attacks.
Attackers with local code execution can read kernel memory.
Only acceptable on a fully isolated gaming machine with no untrusted code ever executed.

options.title: No — keep CPU mitigations
options.desc: Spectre and Meltdown protections remain active. Slight syscall overhead.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep CPU mitigations.

desktopQuestions.label: Vulnerable Driver Blocklist
desktopQuestions.title: Disable the Vulnerable Driver Blocklist?
desktopQuestions.desc: The Vulnerable Driver Blocklist prevents loading of known-vulnerable drivers. performance research notes this is needed for the XHCI IMOD (Interrupt Moderation) tweak — the relevant USB driver is on the blocklist. Only disable if you need a specific blocked driver.
desktopQuestions.note: HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\CI\Config — VulnerableDriverBlocklistEnable = 0. Applies security.disable-vulnerable-driver-blocklist.

options.title: Yes — disable the blocklist
options.desc: Known-vulnerable drivers can load. Required for certain USB/XHCI tuning tools. High security risk.

warnings: Known-vulnerable drivers are a primary vector for privilege escalation and ransomware.
Only disable if you specifically need a driver that is on the blocklist and you trust its source.

options.title: No — keep the blocklist
options.desc: Windows blocks loading of drivers with known vulnerabilities.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep the Vulnerable Driver Blocklist.

desktopQuestions.label: Windows Update
desktopQuestions.title: Fully block Windows Update?
desktopQuestions.desc: performance research documents blocking Windows Update as a way to prevent unexpected reboots and background bandwidth usage. It explicitly marks this as a SECURITY RISK with default FALSE. Blocking updates leaves your system without security patches indefinitely.
desktopQuestions.note: Applies security.disable-update-asap and system.defer-feature-updates. Full block additionally sets the WU service to disabled and blocks the WU firewall rules.

options.title: Allow updates — keep Windows Update
options.desc: Security patches and feature updates install normally. Recommended for all users.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep Windows Update fully active.

options.title: Defer — pause feature updates, allow security patches
options.desc: Feature updates are deferred 365 days. Security patches still install. Auto-restart is suppressed during active hours.

warnings: Feature updates will not install for up to 365 days. Security patches still apply.

options.title: Block — disable all Windows Update
options.desc: Windows Update service is disabled. No patches of any kind will install until you manually re-enable it.

warnings: You will receive no security patches. Known vulnerabilities will remain unpatched indefinitely.
performance research explicitly marks this default: FALSE and labels it a SECURITY RISK.
Only appropriate for air-gapped or isolated gaming machines.
